findings.title: Self-granting privilege path
findings.business_risk: The agent can both request and directly grant application access, creating a self‑granting privilege path that bypasses segregation of duties. This enables unauthorized or excessive access to be provisioned without independent review, exposing all applications the agent can request to potential confidentiality, integrity, and availability breaches.

evidence.detail: holds this effective-access combination
AccessBot has a direct grant of grant_access.
AccessBot has a direct grant of request_access.

findings.recommended_action: Separate the request and grant functions by revoking the agent's direct grant of the grant_access tool, enforce an independent approval workflow for access grants, and apply least‑privilege controls to the agent.
findings.control_mapping: Identity governance — segregation of duties (access request versus access grant)

findings.title: Critical fraud path: create vendor and approve payment
findings.business_risk: InvoiceBot possesses both the create_vendor and approve_payment tool grants, giving a single automated agent the ability to add new vendors and subsequently authorize payments to those vendors. This concentration of authority creates a direct fraud pathway where a malicious or compromised bot could generate fictitious vendor records and approve disbursements without independent oversight, exposing the organization to unauthorized financial loss and SOX ITGC compliance violations.

evidence.detail: holds this effective-access combination
InvoiceBot has a direct grant of approve_payment.
InvoiceBot has a direct grant of create_vendor.

findings.recommended_action: Implement segregation of duties by revoking one of the conflicting grants from InvoiceBot (e.g., remove approve_payment or create_vendor), introduce a separate approval workflow that requires an independent human reviewer, and enforce monitoring of vendor creation and payment approval activities.
findings.control_mapping: SOX ITGC — segregation of duties (vendor creation versus payment approval)

findings.title: Critical ghost-employee fraud path
findings.business_risk: PayrollBot possesses both the add_employee and run_payroll privileges, enabling it to create employee records and immediately trigger payroll payouts. This single point of control creates a critical ghost‑employee fraud pathway, exposing the organization to unauthorized disbursements, financial loss, and SOX ITGC segregation‑of‑duties violations.

evidence.detail: holds this effective-access combination
PayrollBot has a direct grant of add_employee.
PayrollBot has a direct grant of run_payroll.

findings.recommended_action: Revoke one of the conflicting privileges from PayrollBot to enforce segregation of duties (e.g., restrict add_employee to a separate provisioning system). Implement dual‑approval controls for payroll execution, conduct periodic access reviews, and monitor for anomalous employee‑creation and payroll‑run activity.
findings.control_mapping: SOX ITGC — segregation of duties (employee setup versus payroll execution)

findings.title: Critical data-exfiltration path
findings.business_risk: SupportBot possesses both read access to customer PII and the ability to send external email, creating a direct path for unauthorized disclosure of personally identifiable information. If exploited, this could lead to regulatory violations, loss of customer trust, reputational harm, and potential financial penalties.

evidence.detail: holds this effective-access combination
SupportBot has a direct grant of read_customer_pii.
SupportBot has a direct grant of send_external_email.

findings.recommended_action: Restrict SupportBot's permissions to the minimum required for its case‑resolution function. Remove the direct grant to read_customer_pii or isolate it behind a controlled data‑access service, and limit or mediate the send_external_email capability with approval workflows and outbound email monitoring. Implement segregation of duties and continuous audit of privileged tool assignments.
findings.control_mapping: Data protection — least privilege and controlled external egress

owasp_mcp.title: Tool Poisoning
owasp_mcp.relevance: A compromised or misleading tool can steer an agent toward unintended data handling or external actions.

owasp_mcp.title: Software Supply Chain Attacks & Dependency Tampering
owasp_mcp.relevance: A compromised MCP dependency can add covert external egress to an otherwise trusted workflow.

real_world_incident.title: Supabase MCP stored prompt-injection scenario
real_world_incident.relevance: Supabase documented a scenario where stored instructions led an MCP-connected agent to read private database data and write it into an attacker-visible field. It illustrates this read-to-egress risk class; it is not evidence that this fleet uses Supabase.

real_world_incident.title: Malicious postmark-mcp package backdoor (v1.0.16)
real_world_incident.relevance: Postmark reported that an impersonating npm package silently BCC'd email to an external server. It is an analogous tool-poisoning and supply-chain incident, not evidence that this fleet installed the package.

findings.title: Unused standing access: 2 direct grants
findings.business_risk: ReportBot holds standing privileges to delete records and export data that are not required for its stated function of reading analytics and generating weekly reports. If the agent or its host environment were compromised, an attacker could leverage the unused delete_records grant to erase operational data or the export_data grant to exfiltrate sensitive analytics, potentially causing data loss, regulatory non‑compliance, and disruption of reporting processes.

evidence.detail: usage log contains no invocation of these direct grants: delete_records, export_data
ReportBot has a direct grant of delete_records.
ReportBot has a direct grant of export_data.

findings.recommended_action: Revoke the unused delete_records and export_data grants from ReportBot, re‑align its permissions with the principle of least privilege, and implement periodic access certification to ensure granted rights match actual usage.
findings.control_mapping: Least privilege — access certification using granted versus used access

findings.title: Delegated payment-approval blast radius
findings.business_risk: This agent does not hold payment approval directly, but it can reach an agent that does through delegation. Its effective access therefore includes authority to authorize disbursements, creating a confused-deputy path.

evidence.detail: reaches approve_payment only through delegation to finance_bot
chief_of_staff_bot can delegate to finance_bot.
finance_bot is the direct grant holder reached through chief_of_staff_bot -> finance_bot.
ChiefOfStaffBot effectively reaches approve_payment through chief_of_staff_bot -> finance_bot.

findings.recommended_action: Remove or constrain the delegation link to the payment-approving agent. If delegation is necessary, expose a narrowly scoped workflow action rather than the delegate's general approval authority.
findings.control_mapping: Identity governance — effective access review and least privilege

findings.title: Delegated payment-approval blast radius
findings.business_risk: Compromise of ExecBriefingBot (exec_briefing_bot) provides an indirect path to the approve_payment tool via chief_of_staff_bot and finance_bot, enabling unauthorized payment approvals and potential financial loss.

evidence.detail: reaches approve_payment only through delegation to finance_bot
exec_briefing_bot can delegate to chief_of_staff_bot.
chief_of_staff_bot can delegate to finance_bot.
finance_bot is the direct grant holder reached through exec_briefing_bot -> chief_of_staff_bot -> finance_bot.
ExecBriefingBot effectively reaches approve_payment through exec_briefing_bot -> chief_of_staff_bot -> finance_bot.

findings.recommended_action: Restrict delegation chains for payment‑related tools, enforce least‑privilege assignments, and conduct an access‑review to ensure only authorized agents hold direct grant to approve_payment. Consider adding approval workflows that require multi‑person sign‑off before payment execution.
findings.control_mapping: Identity governance — effective access review and least privilege

findings.title: Delegated payment-approval blast radius
findings.business_risk: Because SummaryBot can delegate to finance_bot, which directly holds the approve_payment grant, any request processed by SummaryBot can be escalated to execute payment approvals. This creates a privilege‑escalation blast radius that extends the payment‑approval capability beyond its intended owners, exposing the organization to unauthorized disbursements, financial loss, and compliance violations.

evidence.detail: reaches approve_payment only through delegation to finance_bot
summary_bot can delegate to finance_bot.
finance_bot is the direct grant holder reached through summary_bot -> finance_bot.
SummaryBot effectively reaches approve_payment through summary_bot -> finance_bot.

findings.recommended_action: Restrict or remove the delegation edge from SummaryBot to finance_bot, enforce least‑privilege principles for both agents, and implement an access‑review process that validates any delegation paths to high‑risk tools such as approve_payment. Add monitoring and alerts for any payment‑approval actions initiated via delegated agents.
findings.control_mapping: Identity governance — effective access review and least privilege

owasp_mcp.title: Privilege Escalation via Scope Creep
owasp_mcp.relevance: Delegated authority can turn a narrowly scoped agent into a higher-impact actor through effective access.

real_world_incident.title: Invariant Labs GitHub MCP toxic agent flow
real_world_incident.relevance: Invariant demonstrated an untrusted GitHub issue coercing an MCP-connected agent to move private repository data into a public pull request. It illustrates how composed authority paths can exceed an agent's apparent role.

findings.title: Ownerless agent has no accountable reviewer
findings.business_risk: The LegacyBot agent operates without an assigned owner, meaning no accountable reviewer can certify its access or monitor its activity. Any actions the agent takes against the retired product archive—such as data retrieval, modification, or deletion—occur without oversight, exposing the organization to unauthorized data exposure, integrity loss, and compliance violations.

evidence.detail: owner is null in the fleet inventory

findings.recommended_action: Assign a qualified owner to LegacyBot and enroll the agent in the access certification process to ensure accountable review and ongoing monitoring of its permissions and activities.
findings.control_mapping: Accountability — named owner required for agent access certification

findings.title: AI-generalized toxic capability combination: Read CRM account context + Send email outside the company
findings.business_risk: The SalesBot agent can read CRM account context and directly send emails to external recipients. This combination enables the potential exfiltration of customer data and unauthorized personalized outreach, creating a risk of data leakage, regulatory non‑compliance, and reputational harm if CRM information is disclosed outside the organization.

evidence.detail: holds the cited effective-access combination.
SalesBot has a direct grant of read_crm.
SalesBot has a direct grant of send_external_email.

findings.recommended_action: Implement segregation of duties by removing the direct grant for external email from agents with CRM read access, require an approval workflow for outbound communications, and monitor outbound email logs for anomalous activity.

owasp_mcp.title: Tool Poisoning
owasp_mcp.relevance: A compromised or misleading tool can steer an agent toward unintended data handling or external actions.

owasp_mcp.title: Software Supply Chain Attacks & Dependency Tampering
owasp_mcp.relevance: A compromised MCP dependency can add covert external egress to an otherwise trusted workflow.
